data.title: Caesars Entertainment most shorted S&P 500 consumer discretionary stock in May; Amazon least shorted
data.description:
Average short interest across the S&P 500 consumer discretionary stocks has increased to 2.71% of shares float in end-May, from 2.67% a month earlier.

Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY) has been up 16.7% over the year, as compared to the 9.2% rise in the S&P500 index over the same period.

Stocks with the largest and least short positions

Caesars Entertainment (CZR) was the most shorted stock at 12.82% of shares float, followed by MGM Resorts International (MGM) with short interest of 10.27% and Pool (POOL) with 9.32%, respectively. 

Amazon.com (AMZN) was the least shorted stock, with 61.71M shares sold short or 0.65% of the shares float, followed by Home Depot (HD) and Garmin (GRMN) with short interest of 0.83% and 1.15%, respectively.

Industry Analysis

Average short interest as a percentage of floating shares

 

Other Specialty Retail was the most shorted industry within the sector, with 8.73% short interest as of the end of May, higher from 7.61% at the end of April.

Casinos and Gambling stocks took the second spot with 8.16% short interest as of May end, compared to 7.85% a month ago.

Bets against the Apparel, Accessories and Luxury Goods sector as a whole, however, have declined at the end of May, compared at April-end.

What Quantitative Measures say

Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating system gives Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY) a Hold rating, with 2.93 score.

The ETF has a strong liquidity grade of A+ and momentum grade of also C+. Notably, the ratings' system sees risk as a threat and assigns the ETF a grade of D in this category.

data.title: Analysts say Iran unlikely to shut Strait of Hormuz, one of the world's most vital oil arteries
data.description:
Israel's air strikes on Iran are unlikely to cause a major disruption to oil supply, and market analysts believe a full-scale shutoff of oil flows by closing the Strait of Hormuz is unlikely, perhaps even physically impossible.

Crude oil prices climbed 7% on Friday after Israel launched widescale strikes against Iran, targeting nuclear facilities and missile factories, raising the possibility of a wider war in the Middle East that could even lead to a worst-case scenario involving blockades in the Strait of Hormuz that could push prices above $100/bbl.

Some 20% of the world's total oil consumption passes through the strait, or ~20M bbl/day of oil, condensate and fuel.

Iran has threatened to shut the Hormuz waterway in the past but never acted upon it, with analysts saying closing it off would be a steep logistical challenge.

"Let's be real about the Strait of Hormuz. First of all, most of it is in Oman, not in Iran. Number two, it is wide enough that the Iranians cannot close it," Anas Alhajji, managing partner at Energy Outlook Advisors, told CNBC.

Alhajji said economics would be an equally important factor, since China is the top importer of Iranian oil, reportedly accounting for more than 75% of Iran's oil exports, and China is Iran's second largest overall trade partner.

Iran's friends would "suffer more than their enemies [from a shutdown]... so it's very hard to see that happening," Alhajji said.

"Iran's economy heavily relies on the free passage of goods and vessels through the seaway, as its oil exports are entirely sea-based. Finally, cutting off the Strait of Hormuz would be counterproductive to Iran's relationship" with China, J.P. Morgan analysts said.

"It is our understanding that it would be extremely difficult for Iran to close the strait for an extended period given the presence of the U.S. Fifth Fleet in Bahrain," RBC Capital Markets' head of global commodity strategy Helima Croft wrote.

Iran has been severely weakened during the past year, but with its back to the wall, the regime could turn to an attack on the strait as a last-ditch effort to remain in power.

In any event, heightened Middle East tensions come at a time of ample global oil supply, and rising production in the U.S., Brazil, Canada, and other non-OPEC countries has reduced the Middle East's global market share in recent years, which could help mitigate if not fully offset any supply disruption.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH)

data.title: Amazon to invest $13B in data center infrastructure in Australia over five years
data.description:
U.S. tech and e-commerce giant Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) on Saturday said it would invest AUD 20B ($12.97B) to expand data center infrastructure in Australia by 2029.

As part of the plans, Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) said it would invest in three new solar farms in the Australian states of Victoria and Queensland, and would commit to buying a combined capacity of more than 170 MW across the farms.

"This is the largest investment our country has seen from a global technology provider, and is an exciting opportunity for Australia to build AI capability using secure, resilient infrastructure," Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said in a statement.

The Australian plans come just days after Amazon (AMZN) announced a combined $30B investment in data centers in the U.S. states of North Carolina and Pennsylvania.

data.title: Israel says striking military targets in Tehran, intercepting more Iranian missiles
data.description:
Israel's military on Saturday said the air force was carrying out strikes against military targets in Iran's capital Tehran. Meanwhile, Iran's state news agency said more missiles had been launched at Israel.

The conflict has been escalating since Israel late on Thursday hit Iran with a series of what it called "preemptive" strikes against nuclear sites and military targets. Iran responded with waves of missiles on Friday.

"While the IDF is operating to intercept missiles launched from Iran, the IAF is currently striking military targets in Tehran," Nadav Shoshani, international spokesperson for the Israel Defense Forces (IDF), said on X (formerly Twitter). 

Iran's state news agency, the Islamic Republic News Agency (IRNA), said that a new round of missile attacks had been started against Israel, adding that targets in the Israeli port city of Haifa had been hit.

Israel's military earlier on Saturday said it had eliminated nine of Iran's senior nuclear scientists and experts, a claim that was refuted by the spokesperson of Iran's atomic organization.

The International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) has confirmed that Israeli airstrikes have hit Iran's Natanz and Esfahan nuclear sites.           

Oil prices (CL1:COM)(CO1:COM) have spiked in response to the conflict, U.S. stocks (SP500) have tumbled, and investors have flocked to traditional safe-haven asset gold (XAUUSD:CUR).  

Here are some Israel-focused exchange-traded funds of interest: (NYSEARCA:EIS), (BATS:IZRL), (NYSEARCA:ISRA), and (NYSEARCA:ITEQ).

And here are some ETFs that track the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).  

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: U.S. firms hungry for crypto, starved for clarity: Coinbase survey
data.description:
More than half of Fortune 500 companies are building blockchain projects and stablecoin usage is surging, though Coinbase (NASDAQ:COIN) noted crypto's next leap needs clear U.S. rules.

Six in 10 Fortune 500 executives reported their companies are actively working on blockchain initiatives, crypto exchange Coinbase (NASDAQ:COIN) said in its Q2 2025 State of Crypto survey. Small and medium-sized businesses are bullish, too, with one-third now using crypto -- double last year's share.

What's more, over 80% of crypto-aware SMBs want to put stablecoins like tether (USDT-USD) to work, and the share of F500 firms eyeing stablecoins has more than tripled since 2024's findings.

"2025 is shaping up to be the breakout year for stablecoins," Coinbase said in the report.

Indeed, these price-stable digital assets are becoming more mainstream as demand is surging for their role in trading and global payments. With U.S. President Donald Trump's pro-crypto stance, Congress is advancing crypto legislation to set a clear regulatory framework for the space, and companies like Circle (CRCL) -- the firm behind stablecoin USDC (USDC-USD) -- have made eye-popping market debuts.

Also, some of the U.S.'s largest retailers, including Walmart (WMT) and Amazon (AMZN) are reportedly exploring uses for stablecoins and possibly issuing their own tokens.

Stablecoin supply has surged 54% over the past year, according to the survey, which gathered findings from 100 F500 executives and 251 decision-makers at SMBs in April. The survey was conducted by third-party research firms.

Last year, the annual stablecoin transfer volume hit $27.6T, exceeding a combined volume of payment network titans Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA) by more than 7.68%, Coinbase noted, "highlighting their realized potential for businesses as a faster, cheaper, and more scalable solution for cross-border payments."

While the U.S. may be well on its way to making it the global center for digital asset innovation, reversing a tough and unclear regulatory environment under the Biden administration, 90% of F500 leaders said the U.S. needs comprehensive crypto regulation in order for crypto to realize all it can offer.

In any case, consumers, institutions and SMBs have found that stablecoins, in particular, are a key tool for addressing their "biggest financial pain points," such as fee and transaction processing, the survey said. "The rise of onchain activity and accelerating global adoption signal a fundamental shift in the evolution of money, with stablecoins emerging as a primary catalyst, powering real-world use cases and driving the next wave of digital financial integration."

Seeking Alpha contributor ARK Investment Management noted that stablecoin supply has jumped over 20% to $247B year-to-date, surpassing 1% of the U.S. M2 money supply.

data.title: Over 170 U.S. companies expected to announce job cuts in June
data.description:
U.S. white-collar workers have been feeling the heat of a turbulent economy since the start of 2025.

With increasing pressure to cut down cost cuts in the face of uncertain economic conditions, mostly brought on by President Donald Trump’s trade policies, corporates are relying on sweeping head count reduction. However, layoffs in the tech sector have moderated so far in 2025, for the second year running.

The biggest layoffs of the year took place at Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) in April, when the chipmaker announced plans to cut over 20% of its workforce, amounting to roughly 22,000 jobs, as part of an effort to streamline management and reduce bureaucracy. Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) was next in line after the tech giant reduced its global workforce by 3% or about 6,000 employees.

 

 

Layoffs in June

Going forward, in June, over 170 companies are expected to announce job cuts, according to WARNTracker.com, a website that tracks upcoming redundancies based on WARN notices filed by companies. The Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification Act of 1988 requires employers performing large layoffs or office closures to give at least 60 days' notice to employees and state officials ahead of mass layoffs or plant closures.

As per the website, JPMorgan (NYSE:JPM), Morgan Stanley (NYSE:MS) and Wells Fargo (NYSE:WFC) are among the companies implementing layoffs in the finance sector. Morgan Stanley will start eliminating roles in its New York offices starting June 17.

Meanwhile, JPMorgan has notified authorities in New Jersey that it will let go of about 100-250 employees, effective June 23. Wells Fargo may reduce a small part of its workforce in Iowa starting mid-June.

Walmart (NYSE:WMT), Coca-Cola (NYSE:KO) and eBay (NASDAQ:EBAY) have been listed among consumer goods firms cutting jobs.

As per a Reuters report in May, Walmart planned to cut around 1,500 jobs as part of a broader restructuring. This would impact teams across its global tech division, U.S. e-commerce fulfillment centers, and its advertising arm, Walmart Connect, the report added.  As per WARNTracker.com, the retail chain would slash 50-100 positions in California and 250-500 positions in New Jersey beginning June 13.

Coca-Cola and eBay employees may witness job cuts in California and New York offices respectively later this month.

Procter & Gamble (NYSE:PG) also announced plans to eliminate 7,000 jobs over the next two years as part of a broad cost-reduction strategy.

In the healthcare sector, Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) and Novartis (NYSE:NVS) made a few job cuts this month. While Pfizer has laid off 50-100 employees in California, Novartis may make a few cuts towards the end of the month.

Chevron (NYSE:CVX), United Parcel Service (NYSE:UPS), and FedEx (NYSE:FDX) are some of the major companies making cuts in the industrial and energy sector. Other companies reducing headcount this month include Sodexo (OTCPK:SDXOF), NBCUniversal, Raytheon Technologies and Whirlpool (NYSE:WHR).

data.title: Blackstone reportedly in talks to buy Allegiant Travel’s Sunseeker Resort
data.description:
Blackstone (NYSE:BX) is conducting discussions to acquire Sunseeker Resort, owned by Allegiant Travel (NASDAQ:ALGT), Bloomberg News reported, citing people with knowledge of the matter.

The talks between the two firms are ongoing, and they could fall apart, the people said, asking for anonymity as information hasn’t been made public. Details about a potential deal size were not available. 

Last month, the private equity firm listed several entities, including Sunseeker Resort Owner LLC, with the Florida Division of Corporations.

The 785-room resort, built in 2023 at a $720M cost in Port Charlotte, Florida, has been a drain on Allegiant’s (NASDAQ:ALGT) financials amid construction delays due to the pandemic and hurricane-driven damages.

In July 2024, Allegiant, the owner of Allegiant Airlines, engaged hospitality asset management firm Prospect Hotel Advisors to conduct a strategic review of the hotel, which it built to diversify its operations away from travel services.

data.title: Trending stocks in the week as Middle East tensions hit stock market
data.description:
U.S. equities ended the week in the red as escalating geopolitical tensions between Israel and Iran unsettled markets. On Friday, the S&P 500 fell 1.1%, the Dow dropped 770 points (1.8%), and the Nasdaq slid 1.3%, erasing earlier weekly gains

Over the full week, the S&P 500 lost 0.4%, the Dow 1%, and Nasdaq Composite 0.8%.

Friday’s sell-off was driven by Israel’s airstrikes on Iran targeting nuclear sites, and Iran’s retaliatory strikes, which sharply heightened geopolitical fears.

Oil prices spiked as investors braced for potential disruptions near the Strait of Hormuz. Treasury yields rose on inflation worries, and investors flocked to safe-haven assets like gold and U.S. bonds.

Looking ahead, traders are eyeing next week’s Federal Reserve meeting, which is expected to hold rates steady, while weighing the impact of oil volatility and ongoing geopolitical uncertainty on future market direction.

Amid the volatility seen this week, these were some of the trending stocks:

Boeing (BA) faced renewed scrutiny after one of its 787 Dreamliners crashed in Ahmedabad, India, on Thursday. The London-bound Air India flight, carrying 242 passengers, went down shortly after takeoff, striking a residential area. The incident has once again raised concerns over the safety of Boeing’s aircraft.

Micron Technology (MU) stock was in focus as the memory chipmaker said it would spend about $200B on U.S. semiconductor manufacturing and research and development, in another example of a big company pledging domestic investments following U.S. President Donald Trump's return. The new investment will create an estimated 90,000 direct and indirect jobs, the company said.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), the primary chip supplier to Nvidia and Apple, reported a 39.6% year-over-year rise in May revenue as clients rushed to stockpile chips amid growing trade tensions.

IBM (IBM) stock was in spotlight after the company unveiled plans to build what it calls the "world's first large-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computer." The tech giant plans to deliver the IBM Quantum Starling by 2029 after building it in a new data center in New York. Starling is "expected to perform 20,000 times more operations than today's quantum computers," IBM said.

Chewy (CHWY) stock fell despite solid Q1 earnings from the online retailer of pet food, products and supplies. “We delivered topline growth exceeding the high-end of our net sales guidance range, year-over-year growth in active customers, and compelling profitability and free cash flow generation," top boss Sumit Singh said. The company's current quarter guidance beat expectations, and its fiscal full-year revenue outlook was unchanged.

Oracle (ORCL) rose on Wednesday after the IT giant reported fiscal fourth-quarter results that topped estimates and offered a robust outlook for fiscal 2026.

GameStop (GME) fell after the company posted mixed FQ1 results, with revenue down 16.9% Y/Y despite swinging to a net income of $44.8M from a loss of $32.3M a year ago. The company also announced plans to offer $1.75 billion aggregate principal amount of 0.00% convertible senior notes due 2032 in a private offering.

Air mobility stocks, including Joby Aviation (JOBY) and Archer Aviation (ACHR), extended gains on Monday after President Donald Trump signed an executive order to boost American development, commercialization, and export of drones. The order establishes an electric Vertical Takeoff and Landing (eVTOL) integration pilot program to accelerate deployment of vertical operations in the U.S., selecting at least five pilot projects for use cases like cargo transport and medical response.

J.M. Smucker (SJM) fell nearly 14% over the week after reporting a mixed FQ4 report, with adjusted gross profit down 9% Y/Y. The company issued a cautious FY2026 outlook, forecasting EPS of $8.50–$9.50 (midpoint $9.00), below the $10.25 consensus, and sales growth of 2%–4%, citing headwinds from tariffs, inflation, regulatory shifts, and changing consumer behaviors.

AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) shares surged to a record high on Friday, continuing a remarkable rally. The stock has skyrocketed 275.4% over the past year and is up 81.8% so far in 2025.

Mastercard (MA) and Visa (V) fell on Friday, following a report that retail giants Amazon and Walmart are exploring stablecoin-based payment systems that could sidestep traditional card networks.

data.title: Key deals this week: BioNTech, UnitedHealth, Qualcomm, Papa John's and more
data.description:
Here's a list of key deals reported across sectors this week:

BioNTech (NASDAQ:BNTX) has agreed to acquire its German rival CureVac (NASDAQ:CVAC) in an all-stock transaction worth nearly $1.25B.

Calavo Growers (NASDAQ:CVGW) has announced that it received an unsolicited, non-binding proposal to acquire all outstanding shares at $32.00 per share.

Automotive supplier Dana (NYSE:DAN) on Wednesday said it would sell its off-highway business to Allison Transmission (NYSE:ALSN) for $2.7 billion.

Brown & Brown (NYSE:BRO) has entered into an agreement to acquire RSC Topco, the holding company for insurance brokerage Accession Risk Management Group.

UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH) is considering four non-binding bids for its Banmedica unit for about $1B, as the largest U.S. health insurer doubles down on efforts to exit Latin America.

PMGC Holdings (NASDAQ:ELAB) announced on Monday the signing of a non-binding letter of intent to acquire a U.S.-based, cash-flow positive electronics contract manufacturing company with over 40 years of operational history.

IonQ (NYSE:IONQ) has signed a definitive agreement to acquire Oxford Ionics for $1.075 billion.

Qualcomm (NASDAQ:QCOM) is acquiring British semiconductor company Alphawave IP (OTCPK:AWEVF) at an implied enterprise value of about $2.4B to boost its AI technology portfolio.

Forward Air (NASDAQ:FWRD) +10.8% post-market Friday following a report that private equity firms have expressed interest in acquiring the trucking company.

Papa John's (NASDAQ:PZZA) shares jumped as much as 15% on Wednesday amid reports that Apollo Management (NYSE:APO) and a Qatari investment fund had made a potentially lucrative bid for the pizza chain.

data.title: Short sellers lower bets against S&P 500 financial stocks
data.description:
Average short interest across S&P 500 financial (NYSEARCA:XLF) stocks posted a moderate 4-basis-point decline to 1.78% of shares float in May, compared to 1.82% in the prior month. The prior month short interest has been revised from 1.77% amid inclusion of Coinbase Global (COIN) and exclusion of Discover Financial Services (DFS) in S&P 500 financial index (SP500-40). The number of shares shorted in May fell to 847.28M from 867.58M at the end of April.
 
The S&P 500 financial index (SP500-40) rose 4.29% in the last month, compared to 6.15% gain recorded by S&P 500.

Short interest, which could be an indicator of pessimism, calculates the number of shares sold against the company float. (Short Interest % = Number of Shares Sold Short ÷ Stock Float).

Most shorted stocks:

Franklin Resources (BEN) - 9.33% vs 9.45% last month.

Apollo Global Management (APO) - 6.77% vs 6.92%.

 Coinbase Global (COIN) - 6.23% vs 4.92%.

Erie Indemnity Company (ERIE) - 6.15% vs. 5.85%.

 Regions Financial (RF) - 5.70% vs 5.71%.

Least shorted stocks:

Chubb (CB) – 0.74% vs 0.80% last month.

Mastercard (MA) – 0.75% vs 0.69% last month.

S&P Global (SPGI) – 0.78% vs 0.89.

JPMorgan (JPM) - 0.98% vs 1.01%.

The Charles Schwab (SCHW) - 1.00% vs 1.02%. 

 

Financial Services was at the top of the pack within the financial sector, with 2.10% short interest as of May end, up significantly from 2.01% as of April end.

Capital Markets were the second most shorted industry within the financial sector, with 2.07% short interest as of the end of May, a tick higher than 2.06% in April.

Insurance was the least shorted industry at 1.51% against 1.40% in the prior month.
